title: VectorByte Training Materials 2026
text:
Before attending the workshop, we request that you read the following three papers, as they will provide much of the conceptual framework that we rely on:

MIReAD, a minimum information standard for reporting arthropod abundance data
MIReVTD, a minimum information standard for reporting vector trait data
The Role of Vector Trait Variation in Vector-Borne Disease Dynamics

 




We will be using R for all data manipulation and analyses/model fitting. Any operating system (Windows, Mac, Linux) will do, as long as you have R (version 3.6 or higher) installed.
You may use any IDE/ GUI for R (VScode, RStudio, Emacs, etc). For most people, RStudio is a good option. Whichever one you decide to use, please make sure it is installed and tested before the workshop.
We also host all materials on GitHub, and all students should have a GitHub account.

 



We are assuming familiarity with R basics as well as at least introductory statistics, including up through simple linear regression. If you would like materials to review, we recommend the following from The Multilingual Quantitative Biologist.

R: Biological Computing in R Chapter.
Basic statistics, through linear models: The Multilingual Quantitative Biologist - Basic Data Analyses and Statistics.

title: VectorByte Training Materials 2026
section: Introduction to Data Types and Sources
text:
Introduction to Data Types and Sources

Lecture Slides

title: VectorByte Methods Training
section: Why is Data/Code Curation and Management Important?
text:
Why is Data/Code Curation and Management Important?
In order for analyses to be repeatable, data and code first must:

properly organized and documented
accessibly stored and findable, and
ideally, made available to others.

Data obtained with support from public funds (such as NSF or NIH) are usually required to be made available to other scientists and the public.

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text:
Steps to Data Management
There are many steps to obtaining and effectively managing data (right, below). Today we talk about important components that fit in areas (ii) to (v).




Manage Raw Data
Check Data
Store and Curate Data

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text:
1. Manage Raw Data
So you’ve got some “raw” data:

handwritten notes
automatic data logger (this includes sequencing machines, temperature monitors, etc.)
output from simulation

These data should be transferred to an organized electronic format and checked as soon as possible after collection.

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text:
What Electronic Formats?
It’s often easiest to input as a table into a spreadsheet.

But don’t leave it simply as a spreadsheet – save it to a non-proprietary format, like a comma-delimited file (csv).

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text:
How should data be recorded


Input it in the least compact form that you can – you don’t want to lose information!


Usually this means that you want your data to be in a “long” format – but what does this mean?

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text:
Long vs. Wide
Wide formats compress multiple individual samples into single rows instead of using a row for each sampling unit:




WIDE




Type
#
Weight(g)




Carrots
10
35.1


Peppers
30
30.4


Broccoli
5
200.4







LONG




Type
weight
units




Carrot
2.3
g


Pepper
3.2
g


Broccoli
5.8
g


Pepper
2.2
g


Carrot
1.5
g

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text: Metadata

title: VectorByte Methods Training
section: What is in a “row” of data?
text:
What is in a “row” of data?

units separate from measured values
dates
individual measurements when possible
separate columns for all covariates with units and settings recorded separately.

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text: Examples from VectorByte

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text:
2. Check Data
Almost always errors are made when data are being collected or inputted.



Decimal points moved
Digits switched
Missing data are not properly encoded
Instrument errors
Skip some data




As (and after) you input, do some “sanity checks”

count/sum across rows and columns
check for empty fields
visualize your data and look for outliers.

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text:
FAIR Data Practices
One of the most important practices is to ensure that your data will be preserved and eventually made accessible to others. In particular we want our data to be FAIR:

Findable
Accessible
Interoperable
Reproducible

title: About the VectorByte Training Materials 2026
text:
As the VectorByte team has developed these materials, we’ve aimed to provide resources for both guided (during the workshop) and self-led learning. We assume basic familiarity with:

something
something else

We’ve divided the materials into subject matter modules or units. Each module typically consists of four kinds of materials:

slides with presentation of materials
labs/hands-on materials to allow you to practice material in a practical way
data sets to analyze
solutions to exercises, when necessary

We also include links to additional resources/materials/references.
For more information about the goals and approach of VectorByte are available at vectorbyte.org.

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text:
2. Check Your Data
Almost always errors are made when data are being collected or inputted.

Decimal points moved
Digits switched
Missing data are not properly encoded
Instrument errors
Skip some data

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text:
3. Store and Curate Data


Traditionally, the preferred medium for a “permanent” archival copy is acid-free paper. It has some important advantages:

non-proprietary format
doesn’t need electricity to maintain
properly stored, can last hundreds of years.





That’s not the case with many electronic formats…

title: VectorByte Methods Training
text:
Data Storage
Electronic storage requires updating to new hardware every 5-10 years. Storing data in the cloud outsources much of this effort.
Cloud storage of your data (and your code/ analyses) on a remote server in addition to your local machine is a really good idea.
Further, versioning systems let you keep track of changes and recover old versions of your files (in case you mess up and write over them). This includes GIT (that we’ve already seen).
